gist: The yearly slip from Ferrand Bank showing what my easy-access savings pot earned in interest over the tax year, kept in case I ever have to do a tax return. Nothing was taken off for tax.
memorable: earned about £47 in interest
no tax was deducted from it
it was for the Instant Saver account
downloaded it from online banking in mid-April 2026

gist: A messy scan of the American tax paperwork my US bank posts me every year because I live in the UK. It reports the interest my high-yield savings account over there earned, with nothing held back for US tax since I count as a foreign person.
memorable: roughly $196 of interest for the year
zero US tax withheld
from Commonwealth Harbor Bank; they sent a separate one for the checking account too
sent to my London flat address

gist: Invoice for the first yearly boiler check after I moved into the London flat. The engineer found the corrosion-protection chemical in the heating water was low and added more, and pumped the system back up to the right pressure.
memorable: came to £120 including VAT
first service at the new flat, October 2025
same engineer who used to do the old house, D. Okonkwo
Harwood Heating did it

gist: The expensive boiler visit where they finally worked out why the pressure kept dropping — a burst rubber diaphragm inside the expansion vessel, which they swapped out during the annual service. They also flagged that the flue sits right at the minimum allowed distance from a window and suggested a limescale gadget next time.
memorable: just under £300 all in, versus the usual ~£100 service
cause of the recurring pressure loss was a ruptured expansion vessel
a different engineer than usual (S. Varga)
advice about keeping the window shut when the boiler runs for long stretches

gist: The broadband bill with the surprise callout fee. The internet died in mid-January (hub blinking red), an engineer came out and traced it to a broken socket plate inside the flat, so because the problem was my wiring rather than their network I had to pay for the visit.
memorable: £65 engineer charge, pushing the bill to £99.99 instead of the usual £34.99
fault reported 18 January, fixed on the 21st
Loopfibre said I had 30 days to challenge the charge
problem turned out to be inside the flat, not their line
period: February 2026 bill, covering the January fault

gist: The broadband bill that broke the news of the yearly price hike — the monthly cost of my fibre plan going up by £3 from the start of May. Annoyingly the small print meant I couldn't walk away without paying an exit fee, since the rise was baked into the contract.
memorable: price going from £29.99 to £32.99 a month from 1 May 2026
the static IP extra stayed the same price
no penalty-free right to leave
that month's bill itself was still the old £34.99

gist: Scanned council tax bill for the old house in Hampshire covering the year it sat empty with no furniture in it. No discount for it being empty — full whack — plus a warning that leaving it empty over a year could double the charge.
memorable: full-year charge of £1,624.80, band C
ten monthly payments of about £162
billed specifically because the house was unoccupied and unfurnished
Netherhale council, issued March 2024

gist: My first council tax bill at the London flat — a new account starting from when I became liable in August, so it only covered part of the year, and it had the 25% off for living alone applied from day one.
memorable: part-year amount came to £855.23 after the single-person discount
liability started 1 August 2025
paid over eight instalments of about £107
band C flat
period: August 2025 to March 2026 (part of the 2025/26 year)

gist: Printout of my credit report from a couple of months after the move. The score took a knock because of the fresh change of address and the cluster of brand-new accounts (energy, broadband, an investment platform), though six clean years of never missing a payment was working in my favour.
memorable: score was 782 out of 999, rated Good
run in October 2025 by Veracity Score
flagged the recent move and several accounts opened within three months
no court records, no missed payments

gist: Scan of my actual degree certificate from the University of Wexcombe — the fancy parchment with the chancellor's signature. Bachelor's with honours in computer networks, upper second class.
memorable: got a 2:1
BSc in Computer Networks
conferred in July 2015
signed by the chancellor Dame Rosalind Fetterlane

gist: The letter from Wexcombe offering me a uni place back when I was applying from my parents' house in Hampshire. It was conditional — I had to hit a points target from my A-levels including a decent grade in maths or computing.
memorable: needed 280 UCAS points, with at least a C in Maths or Computing
tuition was £8,500 a year for 2012 entry
course started late September 2012 at the Brackenhill Park campus
signed by the admissions tutor, Dr Fenlow

gist: The electricity bill where Harwood Energy owned up to overcharging me on the daily standing fee across the summer, after I'd raised it with them in September. They put a small credit on the bill and apologised.
memorable: credit of £2.12 for the June-to-August overcharge
they'd been billing 51.2p a day when the tariff said 48.9p
I flagged it on 14 September and they thanked me
the bill still came to £68.09

gist: The brutal mid-winter electricity bill that broke the £100 mark, which came with a plain-English section explaining the regulator's quarterly price cap going up from January. That bill covered December so it was still at the old rates, with the dearer ones kicking in the next month.
memorable: £102.21 to pay
341 units used over December
cap and tariff prices rose from 1 January but hadn't hit this bill yet
on the Fair Deal Flex variable tariff with Harwood

gist: The electricity bill from just after my smart meter came back to life — it had been offline so the previous bill was a guess, and this one used a real reading and automatically squared up any difference from the estimate.
memorable: meter reconnected around 3 March and a proper reading was taken end of March
bill was £89.51
nothing for me to do — the correction happened by itself
274 units for the month
